Now when Jesus heard about it He left there in a boat and went <sup>[across to the east side of the lake]</sup> to a deserted place. When the crowds found out <sup>[He was leaving]</sup> they followed Him <sup>[i.e., by traveling around]</sup> on the shore from the <sup>[surrounding]</sup> towns.
When Jesus came out <sup>[of His place of seclusion]</sup> and saw a large crowd, He felt a deep compassion for them and healed their sick people.

When it became evening, the disciples came to Him and said, “This place is deserted and it is getting late. Send the crowds away so they can go into the towns and buy food <sup>[for their supper]</sup>.”
But Jesus said to them, “They do not need to go away; you men give them <sup>[something]</sup> to eat.”
And they replied, “But we have here <sup>[only]</sup> five loaves of <sup>[barley]</sup> bread <sup>[See John 6:9]</sup> and two <sup>[probably smoked]</sup> fish.”
And He said, “Bring the bread and fish here to me.”

He ordered the crowds to recline on the grass. Then He took the five loaves of bread and two fish and, looking up to heaven, asked God’s blessing on the food, broke it <sup>[in pieces]</sup>, then gave it to the disciples to distribute to the crowds.
They all ate until they were full, then gathered up twelve baskets full of the remaining broken pieces.
Those eating numbered about five thousand men, in addition to the women and children.
Then immediately Jesus ordered the disciples to enter the boat and row on ahead of Him to the other side <sup>[i.e., to the west side of Lake Galilee]</sup> until He could send the crowds away.
After He had dispersed them, He went up into the mountain by Himself to pray. When evening had come He was there alone.
<sup>[Meanwhile]</sup> the boat, still some distance from shore, was being severely battered by the waves because of the opposing winds.
Then between three and six o’clock in the morning Jesus came walking to them on the <sup>[surface of the]</sup> lake.
When the disciples saw Him walking on the water they became frightened, saying, “It is a spirit!” and cried out with fear.
Just then Jesus called out to them, “Take courage and do not be afraid; it is I.”
Peter answered Him, “Lord, if it is <sup>[really]</sup> you, ask me to come to you <sup>[walking]</sup> on the water.”
And Jesus replied, “Come <sup>[ahead]</sup>.” So, Peter climbed over the side of the boat and went to Jesus, walking on the water.
But when he saw the <sup>[furious]</sup> wind he became fearful and began to sink, shouting out, “Lord, save me!”
And immediately Jesus stretched out His hand and took hold of him and said, “You have such little faith; why did you doubt?”
And when they <sup>[i.e., both Jesus and Peter]</sup> got up into the boat, the wind <sup>[immediately]</sup> stopped.
Then the disciples in the boat worshiped Jesus, saying, “Truly you are the Son of God!”
And when they had <sup>[finally]</sup> crossed over <sup>[i.e., to the west side of the lake]</sup>, they landed at the region of Gennesaret.
When the people there recognized Jesus, they sent to all the surrounding regions and had all who were sick brought to Him.
They begged Him that they might only touch the edge of His robe. And all those who got to touch it were made well.
Then some Pharisees and experts in the Law of Moses came to Jesus from Jerusalem and said,
“Why do your disciples violate the tradition of the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders? For they do not <sup>[ceremonially]</sup> wash their hands before eating <sup>[their meals]</sup>.”
And He answered them, “Why do you also violate the commandments of God by <sup>[following]</sup> your traditions?
For God said, <sup>[Ex. 20:12]</sup>, ‘Give honor to your father and mother,’ and <sup>[Ex. 21:17]</sup>, ‘Whoever says bad things about his father or mother should surely be put to death.’
But you say, ‘Whoever says to his father or mother, I have given to God the money I could have helped you with;’
this person does not honor his father <sup>[or mother]</sup>. You have made God’s message useless by <sup>[thus enforcing]</sup> your traditions.
You hypocrites! Isaiah prophesied accurately about you, saying <sup>[Isa. 29:13]</sup>,
‘These people <sup>[claim to]</sup> honor me with their lips <sup>[i.e., by what they say]</sup>, but their heart is far from <sup>[honoring]</sup> me.
They are worshiping me for nothing <sup>[because they are]</sup> teaching principles that are <sup>[merely]</sup> the requirements of men.’”
Then Jesus called the crowds to come to Him, and He said to them, “Listen and understand.
It is not what enters the mouth of a person that <sup>[spiritually]</sup> corrupts him, but what proceeds out of his mouth that <sup>[spiritually]</sup> corrupts him.”
Then His disciples came and said to Him, “Do you know that the Pharisees were upset <sup>[with you]</sup> when they heard <sup>[you say]</sup> that?”
But He answered and said, “Every plant that my heavenly Father did not plant will be uprooted.
<sup>[So]</sup>, let these Pharisees alone; they are blind leaders. And if a blind person leads a blind person, both of them will fall into a ditch.”
And Peter said to Him, “Explain the parable to us.”
And He replied, “Are you still unable to understand?
Do you not realize that whatever enters a person’s mouth goes into his stomach and <sup>[eventually]</sup> passes out into the toilet?
But the things that come out of a person’s mouth come from his heart. These are what <sup>[spiritually]</sup> corrupt a person.
For evil thoughts, murder, extramarital affairs, sexual immorality, stealing, perjury, slander --- <sup>[all these]</sup> come from a person’s heart,
and are what corrupt him <sup>[spiritually]</sup>. But eating <sup>[a meal]</sup> with <sup>[ceremonially]</sup> unwashed hands does not <sup>[spiritually]</sup> corrupt him.”
And Jesus left there and went into the regions of Tyre and Sidon <sup>[i.e., cities on the northwest coast of Palestine]</sup>.

Just then a Canaanite woman came out of that area <sup>[Note: Mark 7:26 calls her a Syrophoenician Gentile]</sup>, crying, “O Lord, son of David, have pity on me. My daughter is seriously troubled by an evil spirit.”
But He did not say a word to her. His disciples came and begged Him; “Send her away, for she is <sup>[continually]</sup> shouting at us <sup>[as we go along]</sup>.”
He answered them, “I was sent only to the straying sheep of the Israelites.”
But she came and worshiped Him, saying, “Lord, help me.”
And He answered <sup>[her]</sup>, “It is not proper to take bread away from the children and throw it to the dogs.”

In the same way also, when you see all these things happening, you <sup>[should]</sup> know that He <sup>[i.e., Jesus]</sup> is as near as your <sup>[front]</sup> door.
Truly I tell you, the people of this generation will not <sup>[all]</sup> die off before all these things <sup>[i.e., mentioned above]</sup> happen.
<sup>[Although]</sup> the sky and the earth will pass away <sup>[See II Pet. 3:10]</sup>, my words will not pass away <sup>[i.e., they will certainly come true]</sup>.
“But no one knows when that day or hour will come. Not even the angels in heaven or the Son <sup>[of God]</sup> know <sup>[when it will happen]</sup>, but only the Father <sup>[does]</sup>.
As it was during the days of Noah, so it will be when the presence of the Son of man <sup>[arrives]</sup>.

For in those days before the Flood, people were eating and drinking <sup>[i.e., partaking of ordinary meals]</sup>, men were getting married and women were being given away in marriage <sup>[right up]</sup> until the day Noah entered the ship.

And the people <sup>[i.e., of Noah’s day]</sup> did not know <sup>[when it would happen]</sup> until the Flood came and swept them all away. It will be the same way when the presence of the Son of man <sup>[arrives]</sup>.

At that time there will be two men <sup>[working]</sup> in a field; one will be taken <sup>[i.e., to be with the Lord. See I Thess. 4:17]</sup>, while the other one will be left <sup>[i.e., to be punished by the Lord. See II Thess. 1:7-9]</sup>.

Two women will be grinding grain at a millstone <sup>[Note: This was a hand-operated device where two persons sat across from each other and rotated a circular stone over kernels of grain]</sup>; one will be taken, while the other one will be left.
Therefore be alert, for you do not know on what day the Lord will come.
But you should know this: if the owner of a house had known when a thief was coming, he would have been watching so as not to allow his house to be broken into.
Therefore, you also should be ready, for the Son of man will come at a time when you least expect Him.
“Who then, is the trustworthy and sensible slave, to whom his master has assigned the management of his household to provide timely meals for its members?
That slave will be happy when his master returns to find him doing his job <sup>[well]</sup>.
Truly I tell you, he will put that slave in charge of his entire estate.
But if that slave, with evil intent, should think to himself, ‘My master will not be back very soon,’
and then becomes physically abusive to his fellow-slaves, and eats and drinks <sup>[at parties]</sup> with drunkards,
that slave’s master will return on a day that he least expects, and at an unknown hour.
And <sup>[that master]</sup> will whip him to shreds and will turn him over to suffer the same fate as hypocrites do. That fate will involve crying and excruciating pain.”

“Then the kingdom of heaven will be similar to ten bridesmaids who took their lamps and went out to meet the <sup>[bride and]</sup> groom. <sup>[Note: The picture here is that of a newly married couple returning from a festive reception to the groom’s home where they continued celebrating and were welcomed by a late evening wedding party]</sup>
Five of the bridesmaids were foolish and five were sensible.
The foolish ones did not take enough olive oil for their lamps with them,
but the sensible ones took <sup>[extra]</sup> oil in containers, along with their lamps.
Now when the <sup>[bride and]</sup> groom were late <sup>[in arriving]</sup>, the bridesmaids all got drowsy and fell asleep.
Then at midnight someone shouted, ‘Look, the <sup>[bride and]</sup> groom are here. Come on, let us go out and meet them.’
Then all the bridesmaids woke up and trimmed their lamps <sup>[i.e., removed the burnt wicks and replenished their lamps with oil]</sup>.
The foolish ones said to the sensible ones, ‘Give us some of your oil, because our lamps are going out.’
But the sensible ones answered, ‘There might not be enough for us and you, <sup>[so]</sup> instead, you should go to the store and buy some for yourselves.’

And while they were away <sup>[trying]</sup> to buy olive oil, the <sup>[bride and]</sup> groom arrived, and those who were ready <sup>[to welcome them]</sup> entered the <sup>[house where the]</sup> wedding reception <sup>[was being held]</sup> and the door was shut.
After that, the other bridesmaids also came asking, ‘Sir, Sir, <sup>[please]</sup> open the door for us!’
But he answered them, ‘Truly I tell you, I do not know you.’
Therefore, you should be watchful, for you do not know the day or hour <sup>[i.e., when the Son of man will return]</sup>.

“For the kingdom of heaven is similar to a man who planned to go on a trip to a distant country, so called his servants and entrusted to each of them a large sum of money <sup>[Note: The narrative that follows suggests that the man was turning over some of his assets to financial managers to invest for him]</sup>.

He gave one servant five talents <sup>[Note: This ‘talent’ was a large sum of money in the form of a certain weight of silver. It was the equivalent of sixty years of a farm laborer’s wages, or about $1,400,000 in 2005 income]</sup>. He gave two talents to another servant and one talent to another. He gave to each one an amount in keeping with his ability <sup>[to invest it wisely]</sup>, and then left on his trip.
The one with the five talents soon invested the money in business transactions which netted him five more.
In the same way the one who was given two talents made two more.
But the one who was given one talent went and dug a hole in the ground and buried his master’s money.
Now after a long time the master of these servants returned <sup>[from his trip]</sup> and proceeded to go over his accounts with each of them.
The one who had been given five talents came bringing another five and reported, ‘Master, you entrusted me with five talents. Look, I have earned five more with them.’

His master replied, ‘Well done, you are a good and trustworthy servant. You have been trustworthy over a <sup>[relatively]</sup> few things, so I will place you over many things. Come and share in your master’s joy.’
And the one who was given two talents came and reported, ‘Master, you entrusted me with two talents. Look, I have earned two more with them.’

His master said to him, ‘Well done, you are a good and trustworthy servant. You have been trustworthy over a <sup>[relatively]</sup> few things, <sup>[so]</sup> I will place you over many things. Come and share in your master’s joy.’

Then the one who was given <sup>[only]</sup> one talent came and reported, ‘Master, I knew you were a difficult man who reaped where you had not sown, and gathered a harvest where you had not scattered seed,
and I was afraid, so I went away and hid your talent in the ground. Look, you <sup>[can]</sup> have your own talent <sup>[back]</sup>.’
But his master said to him, ‘You wicked, lazy servant. <sup>[Since]</sup> you knew that I reaped where I had not sown and gathered where I had not scattered seed,
you should have therefore deposited my money in the bank <sup>[so]</sup> when I returned, <sup>[at least]</sup> I would have received my own money back, with interest.
Take the one talent away from him, therefore, and give it to the servant who had the ten talents.

For to every person who has <sup>[something]</sup>, more will be given, so he will have plenty. But from the person who has <sup>[virtually]</sup> nothing, even that <sup>[little bit]</sup> he has will be taken away <sup>[from him]</sup>.
Throw out that worthless servant into the <sup>[place of]</sup> outer darkness, where there will be crying and excruciating pain.
“But when the Son of man comes <sup>[back]</sup> in <sup>[all]</sup> His splendor, accompanied by all the angels, then He will sit on His throne of splendor.
And people from all the nations will be assembled in front of Him and He will separate them from one another, like a shepherd separating his sheep from the goats.
He will place the sheep at His right side, but the goats at His left.
Then the King will say to those at His right side, ‘You who are blessed by my Father, come and receive the kingdom which has been prepared for you since the creation of the world,

for <sup>[when]</sup> I was hungry, you fed me; <sup>[when]</sup> I was thirsty, you gave me <sup>[something]</sup> to drink; <sup>[when]</sup> I came <sup>[to you as]</sup> a stranger, you gave me a place to stay;
when I was without adequate clothing, you gave me something to wear; <sup>[when]</sup> I was sick, you came to visit me; <sup>[when]</sup> I was in prison, you came to see me.’

Then those who had done what was right <sup>[i.e., the ones at His right side]</sup> replied, ‘Lord, when did we see you hungry, and feed you? Or thirsty, and give you <sup>[something]</sup> to drink?
And when <sup>[did you come as]</sup> a stranger, and we gave you a place to stay? Or <sup>[when were you]</sup> without adequate clothing, and we gave you something to wear?
And when did we see you sick or in prison and come to visit you?’
And the King will answer them and say, ‘Truly I tell you, since you did all this for one of my least <sup>[significant]</sup> brothers, you did it for me.’

Then He <sup>[i.e., the King]</sup> will say to those at His left side, ‘Go away from me, you who are cursed <sup>[by God]</sup>, into the never ending fire which is prepared for the devil and his angels.
For <sup>[when]</sup> I was hungry, you did not feed me; <sup>[when]</sup> I was thirsty, you did not give me anything to drink;

<sup>[when]</sup> I came <sup>[to you as]</sup> a stranger, you did not give me a place to stay; <sup>[when I]</sup> did not have adequate clothing, you did not give me anything to wear; when I was sick, or in prison, you did not visit me.’
They will reply, ‘Lord, when did we <sup>[ever]</sup> see you hungry, or thirsty, or a stranger, or inadequately dressed, or sick, or in prison, and not provide for your needs?’
Then the Lord will answer them, ‘Truly I tell you, since you did not do it for one of these least <sup>[significant brothers of mine]</sup>, you did not do it for me.’
And these <sup>[i.e., the ones at His left side]</sup> will go away to never ending punishment, but those who did what was right will go to never ending life.”
And it happened when Jesus had finished all this teaching, He said to His disciples,

“You know that the Passover Festival will be celebrated in two more days <sup>[Note: This was the annual Jewish festival commemorating Israel’s deliverance from Egyptian bondage under Moses’ leadership]</sup>, and the Son of man will be turned over to be crucified.”
Then the leading priests and elders of the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> people were gathered in the courtroom of the head priest, named Caiaphas.
They were taking counsel together as to how they might take Jesus by trickery and kill Him.
But they reasoned, “We will not do it during the Festival because it might start a riot among the people.”

Now when Jesus was at Bethany <sup>[Note: This was a small village less than two miles east of Jerusalem]</sup>, in the house of Simon, the man <sup>[who probably had been healed]</sup> of an infectious skin disease,
a woman with an alabaster <sup>[i.e., stone]</sup> jar of very expensive perfume came to Him and poured it on His head as He reclined at the dinner table. <sup>[Note: See Matt. 23:6]</sup>
But when His disciples saw this they were very upset and said, “What is the reason for such waste?
For this perfume could have been sold for a large sum of money and given to poor people.”
But Jesus was aware <sup>[of what they were up to]</sup>, so said, “Why are you bothering this woman? For she has done a kind deed to me.
You will always have poor people with you, but you will not always have me.
For in the act of pouring this perfume on my body she was preparing it for my burial.

Truly I tell you, wherever this Gospel <sup>[message]</sup> is preached in the whole world, what this woman has done <sup>[for me]</sup> will <sup>[also]</sup> be told about, as a memorial of her <sup>[kindness to me]</sup>.”
Then one of the twelve apostles, named Judas Iscariot, went to the leading priests

and said, “What would you give me if I turned Jesus over to you?” And they weighed out thirty silver coins <sup>[Note: This amount was equivalent to about five months of a farm laborer’s wages, or approximately $13,500 in 2005]</sup>, and promised <sup>[See Mark 14:11]</sup> to give him that much money.
So, from then on Judas looked for a <sup>[good]</sup> opportunity to turn Jesus over <sup>[to the Jewish leaders]</sup>.

Now on the first day of the Festival of Unleavened Bread <sup>[i.e., the first day of Passover week]</sup>, Jesus’ disciples came to Him, saying, “Where do you want us to make preparations for you to eat the Passover meal?”

He answered, “Go into the city <sup>[i.e., Jerusalem]</sup> to a certain man and say to him, ‘The Teacher says, My time is near <sup>[i.e., to be turned over to the Jewish leaders]</sup>; I plan to observe the Passover Festival at your house with my disciples.’”
And the disciples did <sup>[just]</sup> as Jesus had asked them, and they made preparations for the Passover meal.
Now when evening came, Jesus was reclining at the dinner table with His twelve apostles.
As they were eating, He said, “Truly I tell you, one of you will turn me over <sup>[to the Jewish leaders]</sup>.”
And they were extremely grieved, and each one of them began saying to Him, “Am I the one, Lord?”
He answered, “The one who dipped his hand with me into the <sup>[sauce]</sup> bowl is the one who will turn me over <sup>[to the Jewish leaders]</sup>.

The Son of man is going <sup>[to die]</sup>, but it is too bad for that person through whom the Son of man is turned over <sup>[to the Jewish leaders]</sup>! It would be better for that man if he had not been born.”
Then Judas, the one who betrayed Him asked, “Am I the one, Rabbi?” Jesus answered Him, “You have said so.”

And as they were eating, Jesus took a <sup>[small]</sup> loaf of bread, asked God’s blessing on it, then broke it and gave pieces to His disciples and said, “Take some and eat it; this is <sup>[i.e., represents]</sup> my <sup>[physical]</sup> body.”
And He took a cup <sup>[i.e., probably wine made from fresh or possibly preserved grape juice]</sup>, gave thanks to God, then passed it to them, saying, “All of you, drink from it;

for this is <sup>[i.e., represents]</sup> my blood of the Agreement <sup>[i.e., between God and mankind]</sup>, which is <sup>[to be]</sup> poured out for many people in order for <sup>[their]</sup> sins to be forgiven.

But I tell you, I will not drink of this fruit of the vine <sup>[i.e., grape juice]</sup> from now on until that day when I will drink it again with you <sup>[i.e., figuratively]</sup> in my Father’s kingdom.”
And after they had sung a hymn, they went up to the Mount of Olives. <sup>[Note: This was a small hill just east of Jerusalem]</sup>

Then Jesus said <sup>[to His disciples]</sup>, “All of you will have doubts about me tonight, for it is written <sup>[Zech. 13:7]</sup>, ‘I will strike down the shepherd and the sheep of the flock will be scattered everywhere.’
But after I am raised up, I will go on ahead of you to Galilee.”
Peter answered Him, “<sup>[Even]</sup> if everyone <sup>[else]</sup> has doubts about you, I never will.”
Jesus said to him, “Truly I tell you, this <sup>[very]</sup> night, before the rooster crows, you will deny <sup>[knowing]</sup> me three times.”
Peter replied to Him, “Even if I have to die with you, still I will not deny <sup>[knowing]</sup> you.” And all <sup>[the rest of]</sup> the disciples said the same thing.

Then Jesus came with His disciples to a place called Gethsemene. <sup>[Note: This was an olive orchard on a hillside just east of Jerusalem]</sup>. He said to them, “You sit here while I go over there and pray.”
He took Peter and the two sons of Zebedee <sup>[i.e., James and John]</sup> with Him, and <sup>[soon]</sup> began to be grieved and very distressed.
Then He said to them, “My soul is extremely grieved, even to <sup>[the point where I could]</sup> die. Stay here and be alert with me <sup>[i.e., in prayer]</sup>.”

And He went on ahead a short distance and fell face-down and prayed, “My Father, allow this cup <sup>[i.e., of suffering]</sup> to pass away from me if it is <sup>[at all]</sup> possible; nevertheless, let it not be what I want, but what You want <sup>[for me]</sup>.”

And <sup>[when]</sup> He came <sup>[back]</sup> to where His disciples were and found them asleep, He said to Peter, “What <sup>[is this]</sup>, could you not remain alert with me <sup>[in prayer]</sup> for one hour?

Be alert and pray, so that you do not give in to temptation; the spirit <sup>[of a person]</sup> is truly willing <sup>[i.e., to do a certain thing]</sup>, but his physical nature is <sup>[often too]</sup> weak <sup>[to carry it out]</sup>.”

He <sup>[then]</sup> went away a second time and prayed, “My Father, if this <sup>[cup of suffering]</sup> cannot pass away unless I drink it <sup>[i.e., experience the suffering]</sup>, <sup>[then]</sup> let what You want be done.”
And He came <sup>[back]</sup> again and found them asleep, because they had not been able to keep their eyes open.
He left and went away from them again and prayed a third time, saying the same words again.
Then He came to His disciples and said to them, “Go ahead and sleep now, and get your rest. See, the time is near and the Son of man is <sup>[about to be]</sup> turned over into the hands of sinners.
Get up, let us be going. Look, the one who will turn me over <sup>[to the Jewish leaders]</sup> is nearby.”

And while He was still speaking, Judas, one of the twelve apostles, came with a large crowd who were carrying swords and clubs. They had come from the leading priests and elders of the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> people.
Now the one who betrayed Him had given them a signal, saying, “Whoever I give a kiss <sup>[of greeting to]</sup>, he is the one, take hold of him.”
So, immediately he went to Jesus and said, “Greetings, Rabbi,” and <sup>[then]</sup> kissed Him <sup>[enthusiastically]</sup>.
And Jesus said to him, “<sup>[My]</sup> friend, <sup>[go ahead]</sup>, do what you came to do.” Then the Jewish officers came and arrested Jesus and began taking Him away.
Just then, one of those who were with Jesus <sup>[i.e., Peter. See John 18:10]</sup>, reached out with his sword and struck the head priest’s servant <sup>[with it]</sup>, shearing off his ear.
Then Jesus said to him, “Put your sword back in your belt again, for all those who use the sword will die by the sword.

Or, do you think that I cannot appeal to my Father and He will immediately send me more than twelve legions of angels <sup>[i.e., Jesus could have called about 72,000 angels to minister to Him. See Matt. 4:11]</sup>?
How <sup>[else]</sup> then should the Scriptures be fulfilled that this must happen <sup>[than what is taking place]</sup>?”

<sup>[Then]</sup> Jesus said to the crowds at that very time, “Have you come out to arrest me with swords and clubs as <sup>[you would apprehend]</sup> a thief? I sat everyday teaching in the Temple, and you did not <sup>[come to]</sup> take me.
But all this has happened so that the Scriptures of the prophets would be fulfilled.” Then all of His disciples left Him and ran away.
And those who had arrested Jesus led Him away to the house of Caiaphas, the head priest, where the experts in the Law of Moses and the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders were gathered.

But Peter followed Him from a distance <sup>[as they were going]</sup> to the courtyard of the head priest. He went inside <sup>[i.e., eventually. See John 18:15-16]</sup> and sat down with the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> officers to see how things would turn out <sup>[i.e., with Jesus’ trial]</sup>.
Now the leading priests and the entire Council <sup>[called “the Sanhedrin”]</sup> looked for false witnesses <sup>[to testify]</sup> against Jesus, in order to put Him to death.

But they did not find any <sup>[i.e., whose stories harmonized. See Mark 14:56]</sup>, even though many false witnesses came <sup>[forward to testify]</sup>. Finally, two <sup>[false witnesses]</sup> came forward
and said, “This man <sup>[i.e., Jesus]</sup> said, ‘I can destroy the Temple of God, and <sup>[then]</sup> rebuild it within three days.’”
The head priest stood up and said to Him, “Have you nothing to answer? What about this charge being made against you by these people?”

But Jesus did not say anything. So, the head priest said to Him, “I command you <sup>[to swear]</sup> by the living God, <sup>[and]</sup> to tell us if you are the Christ <sup>[i.e., God’s specially chosen one]</sup>, the Son of God!”

Jesus replied to him, “You have said so; nevertheless I tell you, from now on you will see the Son of man sitting at the right side of Power <sup>[i.e., God Himself]</sup>, and coming on the clouds of the sky.”

Then the head priest tore at his clothing <sup>[i.e., as an expression of frustration]</sup>, saying, “He has spoken against God <sup>[i.e., by claiming Deity for Himself]</sup>. What additional need do we have for witnesses? Look, you <sup>[yourselves]</sup> have now heard him speaking against God.
What do you people think <sup>[i.e., about such statements]</sup>?” They answered, “He deserves to die.”
Then they spit in His face and hit Him <sup>[with their fists]</sup>, while others slapped Him <sup>[in the face]</sup>,
saying, “Prophesy to us, you ‘Christ.’ Tell us who hit you?” <sup>[Note: By this time Jesus had been blindfolded. See Mark 14:65]</sup>.
Now Peter was sitting outside in a courtyard and a servant-girl approached him and said, “You also were with Jesus, the Galilean.”
But he denied <sup>[it]</sup> in front of all of them, saying, “I do not know what you are talking about.”
And when he had gone out by the gate, another servant-girl saw him and said to those nearby, “This man also was with Jesus of Nazareth.”
And he denied <sup>[it]</sup> again, <sup>[this time]</sup> with an oath, saying, “I do not <sup>[even]</sup> know the man.”
And a little later, those standing nearby came to Peter and said, “<sup>[We know]</sup> for sure, you also are one of them <sup>[i.e., Jesus’ disciples]</sup>, because your accent gives you away.”
Then Peter began to curse and swear, “<sup>[I tell you]</sup>, I do not know the man.” And immediately a rooster crowed.
Then Peter remembered the words Jesus had said, “Before the rooster crows, you will deny <sup>[knowing]</sup> me three times.” And he went out and cried bitterly.
Now when morning came, all the leading priests and the elders of the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> people conferred together against Jesus as to how they might put Him to death.
So, they chained Him, led Him away and turned Him over to Pilate, the <sup>[Roman]</sup> governor.

Then Judas, who had betrayed Him <sup>[to the Jewish leaders]</sup>, when he saw that Jesus had been condemned to die, changed his mind <sup>[i.e., about betraying Him]</sup> and brought back the thirty silver coins <sup>[Note: See Matt. 26:15 for the amount of money involved]</sup> to the leading priests and <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders,
saying, “I have sinned by delivering up an innocent man to die.” But they replied, “What is that to us? It is your affair!”
And he threw down the thirty silver coins in the Sanctuary <sup>[i.e., probably the outer area of the Temple]</sup>, then left and went out and hanged himself.

The leading priests took the silver coins and said, “It is not permissible by the Law of Moses to put this money in the Temple treasury, since it is the price <sup>[paid]</sup> for <sup>[taking]</sup> a life.

So, they conferred together <sup>[and decided]</sup> to buy ‘The Potter’s Field’ with the money <sup>[Note: This was probably an abandoned field where clay had been obtained for making pottery]</sup>, for use in burying people who were not from the local area.
So, that field is called, ‘The Field of Blood’ to this day.

<sup>[Thus the prophecy]</sup> spoken by Jeremiah, the prophet <sup>[Note: The passage actually appears to be Zech. 11:12- 13]</sup>, was fulfilled which said, ‘And they took the thirty silver coins, the amount charged for Him on whom certain Israelites had set a price,
and gave the money for ‘The Potter’s Field,’ as the Lord directed me.”
Now Jesus stood in front of the governor <sup>[i.e., Pilate]</sup>, who asked Him, “Are you the king of the Jews?” Jesus answered him, “You have said so.”
When He was accused by the leading priests and the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders, He made no reply.
Pilate then said to Him, “Do you not hear how many charges the witnesses are bringing against you?”
But He <sup>[again]</sup> made no reply, not even to a single charge, which caused the governor to be greatly amazed.
Now at the <sup>[Passover]</sup> Festival, the governor was accustomed to releasing whatever prisoner the crowd wanted.
There was at that time in custody a notorious prisoner named Barabbas.
Therefore, when the people had gathered together, Pilate asked them, “Whom do you want me to release, Barabbas or Jesus, who is called ‘Christ’?”
For he knew that the reason they had turned Jesus over to him was out of envy.

Then while Pilate was seated in his courtroom of justice, his wife sent <sup>[word]</sup> to him, saying, “You should have nothing to do with that innocent man. For I was very troubled in a dream over him today.” <sup>[Note: That is, she was troubled over a dream she had before waking up that morning]</sup>.
Now the leading priests and <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders persuaded the crowds to ask for Barabbas <sup>[to be released]</sup> and for Jesus to be killed.
But the governor asked them, “Which one of these two men do you want me to release to you?” And they answered, “Barabbas.”
Pilate replied, “Then what should I do to Jesus, who is called ‘Christ’?” They all shouted, “Let him be crucified!”
And he said, “Why <sup>[should he be]</sup>? What wrong has he done?” But they shouted out even louder, “Let him be crucified!”

So, when Pilate saw that he was not convincing anyone, but that a riot was brewing instead, he took water and washed his hands in front of the crowd saying, “I am not responsible for this man’s blood. <sup>[Note: Some ancient manuscripts say “righteous man’s blood”]</sup>. You tend to the matter.”
And all the people answered, “Let his blood be on our hands, and on our children’s <sup>[as well]</sup>.”
Then he released Barabbas to them, but he had Jesus flogged and then turned over to be crucified.
Then the governor’s soldiers took Jesus to Pilate’s headquarters and assembled the entire company <sup>[i.e., about 500 men]</sup> in front of Him.

They stripped off His clothing and placed a scarlet robe on Him <sup>[Note: Mark 15:17 calls this a “purple” robe because in that day, any color with a mixture of “red” in it was often called “purple,” so the use of “scarlet” was appropriate]</sup>.
Then they made a wreath out of thorns and placed it on His head and put a stick in His right hand. They knelt down in front of Him and mocked Him, saying, “Hey, king of the Jews!”
And they spat on Him and took the stick and beat Him on the head with it.
And after mocking Him <sup>[some more]</sup>, they removed the <sup>[scarlet]</sup> robe and put His own clothes back on Him. Then they led Him away to crucify Him.

As they were leaving <sup>[i.e., probably the city of Jerusalem]</sup>, they met a man from Cyrene <sup>[Note: This was a city in the northern African country of Libya]</sup>. His name was Simon and they forced him to go with them so he could carry Jesus’ cross <sup>[i.e., probably only the cross-beam portion]</sup>.
And when they had come to the place called Golgotha, which means “the place of a skull” <sup>[i.e., probably because it was a skull-shaped knoll in appearance]</sup>,
they offered Jesus wine mixed with a bitter drug <sup>[i.e., for the purpose of dulling the pain of crucifixion. Mark 15:23 calls it “myrrh”]</sup>, but when He tasted it He refused to drink it.
And after they had crucified Him, the soldiers divided His clothing among themselves by gambling for them.
Then they just sat there and watched Him <sup>[die]</sup>.
They attached over His head <sup>[i.e., to the upright portion of the cross]</sup> the charge made against Him, which read, “This is Jesus, the king of the Jews.”
They <sup>[also]</sup> crucified two thieves with Him, one at His right side and one at His left.
And those who passed by <sup>[the cross]</sup> shouted abuse at Him and shook their heads <sup>[in derision]</sup>,

saying, “You who <sup>[said you]</sup> would destroy the Temple and rebuild it in three days, save yourself <sup>[from dying]</sup>; if you <sup>[really]</sup> are the Son of God, come down from the cross.”
In the same way the leading priests, experts in the Law of Moses and <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders also mocked Him, saying,

“He saved other people, <sup>[but]</sup> he cannot <sup>[even]</sup> save himself <sup>[i.e., from dying]</sup>. He is ‘the king of Israel;’ let him now come down from the cross and then we will believe in him.
He trusted in God; now let God deliver him, if He wants <sup>[to save him from dying]</sup>, for he said, ‘I am the Son of God.’”
And the thieves who were crucified with Him also heaped the same abuse on Him.
Now from noon until three o’clock in the afternoon there was darkness over all the land.

Then about three in the afternoon Jesus called out in a loud voice, saying, Eli, Eli, lama, sabachthani <sup>[Note: These words were spoken in the commonly used Aramaic language]</sup>, which mean, My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?
And when some of those standing there heard this, they said, “This man is calling for Elijah.”
And immediately one of them ran and got a sponge, soaked it in sour wine and, attaching it to a stick, <sup>[lifted it up]</sup> and offered Jesus a drink.
But the rest of them said, “Let him alone; let us see if Elijah <sup>[really]</sup> comes to save him.”
Then Jesus called out again with a loud voice and <sup>[finally]</sup> gave up His spirit <sup>[to God. See Luke 23:46]</sup>.
Just then the Temple curtain was torn in two from top to bottom, the ground shook, and rocks were split open.
Graves opened up and the bodies of many dead saints <sup>[i.e., God’s holy people]</sup> were raised up <sup>[from the dead]</sup>.
They came out of their graves after Jesus’ resurrection and entered the holy city <sup>[i.e., Jerusalem]</sup> where they appeared to many people.

Now when the military man in charge, and those with him who were watching Jesus, saw <sup>[and felt]</sup> the earthquake and the <sup>[other]</sup> things that happened, they became extremely fearful, saying, “Truly this was the Son of God.”
And many women, who had followed Jesus from Galilee and had ministered to His needs, were watching <sup>[all this]</sup> from a distance.

Among them were Mary from Magdala; Mary, the mother of James and Joseph <sup>[i.e., probably the wife of Clopas. See John 19:25]</sup>; and the mother of James and John, the sons of Zebedee <sup>[i.e., Salome. Mark 15:40]</sup>.
And when evening had come, a rich man from Arimathea named Joseph, who was also one of Jesus’ disciples,
went to <sup>[governor]</sup> Pilate and asked for the body of Jesus. So, Pilate ordered that it be given to him.
Joseph took the body and wrapped it in a clean linen cloth
and placed it in his own newly prepared grave, which he had cut out of <sup>[a ledge of]</sup> rock. Then he <sup>[had]</sup> a huge stone rolled across the entrance of the grave and went away.
Mary from Magdala and the other Mary <sup>[i.e., probably Jesus’ mother. See verse 56]</sup>, stood near the grave site.
Now on the following day, which was the day after the Preparation <sup>[for the Passover Festival]</sup>, the leading priests and Pharisees assembled in front of Pilate
and said, “Sir, we remember what that deceiver said while he was still alive: ‘I will rise <sup>[from the dead]</sup> after three days.’

Therefore, give the order to secure the grave site until the third day, so his disciples will not steal his body and then tell the people ‘He has risen from the dead.’ This last deception would be worse than the first one <sup>[i.e., the claim that Jesus rose from the dead would be worse than the claim that he was the Messiah]</sup>.”
Pilate said to them, “Take <sup>[a group of]</sup> guards <sup>[with you]</sup>; go and make the grave site as secure as you can.”
So, they went and secured the grave site, placing a <sup>[wax]</sup> seal on the stone, while the guards watched.

Now as the Sabbath day ended and the first day of the week <sup>[i.e., Sunday]</sup> approached <sup>[Note: See Mark 16:1-2 for the exact timing]</sup>, Mary from Magdala and the other Mary <sup>[i.e., Jesus’ mother. See Matt. 27:56]</sup> went to look at the grave site.
Now there had been a violent earthquake, for an angel of the Lord had descended from heaven and, <sup>[having]</sup> come to roll away the stone, was <sup>[now]</sup> sitting on it.
He appeared as <sup>[brilliant]</sup> lightning and his clothing was white as snow.
And the guards shook with fear at seeing him, and became as dead men.
The angel said to the women; “Do not be afraid, for I know you are looking for Jesus, who has been crucified.
He is not here, for He has risen just as He said <sup>[He would]</sup>. Come and see the place where the Lord had been lying,

then go quickly and tell His disciples, ‘He has risen from the dead and is <sup>[now]</sup> going on ahead of you into Galilee. You will see Him there.’ Now <sup>[that is all]</sup> I have to tell you.”
So, they left the grave site quickly, with fear and great joy, and ran to give the message to His disciples.
Suddenly Jesus met them and gave them greetings. They approached Him and took hold of His feet <sup>[and knelt]</sup> to worship Him.
Then Jesus said to them, “Do not be afraid. Go and tell my brothers <sup>[i.e., the disciples, that you have seen me]</sup>, so they can head for Galilee where they will see me.”
Now while these women were on their way, some of the guards entered the city and began telling the leading priests everything that had happened.
And when they had assembled with the <sup>[Jewish]</sup> elders and conferred with them, they offered large sums of money to the soldiers,
saying, “We want you to say, ‘Jesus’ disciples came at night and stole his body while we were asleep.’
And if the governor hears about this <sup>[plot]</sup>, we will persuade him <sup>[i.e., not to punish you for negligence]</sup>, so you will not have to worry about that.”

So, they took the money and did as they were instructed. And this story <sup>[i.e., that Jesus’ body had been stolen by His disciples]</sup> was told widely among the Jews, and continues to be told to this day.
But the eleven disciples <sup>[i.e., apostles]</sup> went to the mountain in Galilee where Jesus had ordered them to go.
And when they met Him <sup>[there]</sup>, they <sup>[knelt down]</sup> to worship Him; but some <sup>[still]</sup> had doubts <sup>[i.e., about His resurrection. See John 20:25]</sup>.
Then Jesus came and spoke to them, saying, “I have been given all authority in heaven and on earth.

So, as you go, make disciples out of people from all the nations, then immerse believers <sup>[See Mark 16:15-16]</sup> into the name of <sup>[i.e., to enter a relationship with]</sup> the Father, and the Son and the Holy Spirit.
<sup>[Then]</sup> go on teaching them <sup>[i.e., these new converts]</sup> to obey everything I have commanded you and my presence will be with you always, even to the end of the age.”
